answer:
**A recurring gnomic maxim across these texts is that truth (or truthful speech) is the supreme virtue or duty (*dharma*), superior even to other pious acts like sacrifice or charity.**[^1][^2][^3]

In *Maxims I* (part of the Old English gnomic poetry tradition), truthfulness is upheld as essential to the wise life: those who "hold to their truth with justice" are blessed, protecting their souls amid life's hardships, while the foolish—who ignore their Lord—face sudden death.[^4] This is contextualized within a catalog of social and cosmic wisdoms, where proper speech (e.g., exchanging "sayings" wisely, speaking "counsel") maintains harmony, echoing the poem's broader ethic of restraint and righteousness under God's order.[^5]

In *Ślokāntara* (a Javanese gnomic compilation drawing on Indic sources), the maxim is explicit: "There is no *dharma* greater than truth (*nāsti satyāt paro dharmo*)"; one must not abandon truth (*satyaṁ na lopayet*), as falsehood (*anṛta*) is the greatest sin.[^6] This caps a hierarchy where truth surpasses even hundreds of sons, sacrifices, or lakes/wells (built as pious gifts). Proper speech follows: sweet (*madhura*) or truthful words in context (e.g., to women or scholars), avoiding slander or anger, lest one become a *cāṇḍāla*.[^7]

In the *Manusmṛti*'s closing chapter (Book 12), truthful speech is prescribed as part of *sāttvic* virtue: avoid the "fourfold verbal action" of "harshness, falsehood (*anṛta*), slander... and irrelevant talk," as bad speech binds one to lower rebirths.[^8] Truth aligns with knowledge and *sattva* (purity), yielding heavenly fruits, while lies fuel *tāmasic* delusion and hellish karma.[^9]

**The traditions do not justify the prescription on precisely the same grounds, though all link it to cosmic/social order and postmortem reward.** The Anglo-Saxon poet grounds it in divine ordinance and earthly prudence (e.g., wisdom-exchange fosters peace; truth shields the soul).[^10] *Ślokāntara* emphasizes hierarchical merit: truth's "reward" exceeds material piety, averting endless sin-cycles.[^11] Manu systematizes it karmically: truthful speech purifies *guṇas*, ensuring high rebirths over beastly or hellish ones.[^12] All invoke eschatological stakes (blessing vs. doom), but the OE focus is communal wisdom under God, Indic/Javanese on *dharma*-merit hierarchies.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), `citation_url` from read_chapter.
[^2]: *Ślokāntara*, ch. 1, [p-11d11c](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628-d7a628/01/translation/#p-11d11c).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 12, [p-92d22b](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-92d22b) (contextualizing verbal ethics).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [p-?] (body text: "Wise men protect their souls, they hold to their truth with justice."). Exact para from full annotation.
[^5]: Ibid., [p-84cf09](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09) ("Wise men should exchange sayings").
[^6]: *Ślokāntara*, ch. 1, [p-11d11c](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628-d7a628/01/translation/#p-11d11c).
[^7]: Ibid., [p-e303ea](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628-d7a628/01/translation/#p-e303ea) (sweet words); [p-77e620](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628-d7a628/01/translation/#p-77e620) (allowable lies).
[^8]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 12, [p-92d22b](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-92d22b) (Mn_12.6).
[^9]: Ibid., [p-3e6ead](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-3e6ead) (sins' fruits); sattva chars. [p-a0210c](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-a0210c).
[^10]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [p-84cf09](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^11]: *Ślokāntara*, ch. 1, [p-02777f](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628-d7a628/01/translation/#p-02777f).
[^12]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 12, [p-26ecce](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-26ecce) (guṇas/rebirth).
